What I'm looking to research, and I think this may require a HEAVY research, is to learn how the latest thinkers are handling the discrepancy between using Agile methodologies a few years ago and using the same methodologies today.  For instance, momentum is still using the concepts of stories, sprints, epics, etc.  I only just implemented th concept of features as a way of handling more end-to-end ways of thinking.  There are multiple problems with doing things the old way.  First, the old \"ticket\" or \"story\" or \"task\" way of handling a piece of functionality is extremely fine grained when you're working in a Gen AI capacity.  It's based on the idea of a single developer wanting to do something in about a day or less, and being able to estimate the level of work.  This falls apart totally when it comes to a Gen AI situatioin, that might be able to handle a dozen of those in a couple of hours.  In addition, concepts such as Acceptance Criteria are fine when you're working on a single story a day.  You can carefully evaluate the very low level and technical acceptance criteria for a story because it's probably the only one you're working on that day.  And furthermore, if you find that it was insufficient, a human being can quickly adapt, talking to the product/project manager or stakeholders and updating the specification on the fly, but an AI Agent will not do that.  They will instead implement the non-functional specification exactly as written.  Then you end up with a pristine piece of code that doesn't deliver any value to your users because it doesn't actually work.  It meets the specification, but it lacks the developer checking it and validating the functionality.  The way I've dealt with this is trying to create the E2E validators but so far I've struggled with this not only technically but the prompt engineering required to not get the AI agent to \"cheat\" and just look at code.  So again they are validating the specification against code rather then evaluating behavior against a running app.  The human mind fills in lots of points that the specification leaves out. So while the specifications I'm using are FAR more complete, being generated by the AI to follow a given template and human reviewed, the final result is still worst than what you would have with a much poorer specification and a human brain to fill in all the unstated requirements when he validates the functionality.  So to me, there are two huge gaps, first, the creating of the story appears to be done at a level that is great for a machine but overwhelming for a human.  The machine can get through dozens of these in a day but I cannot possibly read through dozens of extremely low level acceptance criteria speciications without my brain turning to much and missing obvious errors.  On top of that, the specification doesn't actually deliver working functionality.  Instead the specification is correct, the code that delivers the specification is correct, the unit tests are correct, and the specification doesn't deliver anything of value. I've played with the concept of \"Features\" in momentum to fill this gap where a \"Feature\" is mapped to human user value in some way.  So a feature is not DONE until it's delivered that functionality in a user accessible way.  But there are no end of technical challenges with this approach.  AND it all came from my head.  I have a feeling there are lots of smart people out there, Martin Fowler, Thoughtworks, and other people, thinkers and organizations who have see the same problem I have and found workable solutions.  This is what I need the research to find."
